text: All right, so presenting the case of a robotic right colectomy here. This is Adam Rothermel coming to you from the little city, big town of Zanesville, Ohio. We've got a seventy-two-year-old lady with a large tubulobilous adenoma of the cecum presented with some anemia found by one of our excellent gastroenterologists and referred to our practice for resection.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection approach, identifying the ureter visible on the left and the duodenum. The proximal transverse mesocolon is peeled off, revealing the right branch of the middle colic vessel. The short mesentery of the proximal transverse colon is evident, and the right branch of the middle colic vessel is taken. The dissection finalizes the proximal resection margin, creating a mesenteric window at the terminal ileum using the robotic platform.

The surgeon takes down the hepatic flexure, dissecting through the gastrocolic ligament to connect with the medial-to-lateral dissection. The duodenum is swept down and moved laterally towards the hepatic flexure. The robotic stapler with a blue load is used to finalize the hepatic flexure, while the lateral attachments along the white line remain unreleased until the end. The mesentery dissection is still in progress.

The surgeon takes down the lateral attachments of the colon, completing the mobilization after a medial to lateral approach. A blue load stapler is used across the terminal ileum. The specimen is freed and placed above the liver to clear the surgical field for the upcoming anastomosis.

The surgeon uses two 2-0 silk stay sutures to perform a side-to-side isoperistaltic anastomosis. A collotomy and enterotomy are created using scissors. The anastomosis is completed with a white load stapler, and the closure of the common channel is achieved with a 3-0 V-Loc absorbable suture.

The surgeon completes the first layer of sutures in the anastomosis, running imbricating sutures back to overlap the starting point. After breaking the suture halfway, a new suture is placed to continue closure from the back part of the common channel. The two ends are brought together and tied with knots. The stay suture, a duo silk, is used to suspend the bowel against the abdominal wall, providing stability for the anastomosis and placing reinforcement sutures.
The surgeon places the specimen in the pelvis and removes it through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. The omentum is positioned over the anastomosis site.
